coredata.dc:title: Functionalization of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot: A sustainable carbon-based catalyst for the production of cyclic carbonate from epoxide and CO2

coredata.dc:description: A series of organic compounds were successfully immobilized on an N-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD) to prepare a multifunctional organocatalyst for coupling reaction between CO2 and propylene oxide (PO). The simultaneous presence of halide ions in conjunction with acidic- and basic-functional groups on the surface of the nanoparticles makes them highly active for the production of propylene carbonate (PC). The effects of variables such as catalyst loading, reaction temperature, and structure of substituents are discussed. The proposed catalysts were characterized by different techniques, including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), field emission scanning electron microscopy/energy dispersive X-ray microanalysis (FESEM/EDX), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), elemental analysis, atomic force microscopy (AFM), and ultraviolet–visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopy. Under optimal reaction conditions, 3-bromopropionic acid (BPA) immobilized on N-GQD showed a remarkable activity, affording the highest yield of 98% at 140°C and 106 Pa without any co-catalyst or solvent. These new metal-free catalysts have the advantage of easy separation and reuse several times. Based on the experimental data, a plausible reaction mechanism is suggested, where the hydrogen bonding donors and halogen ion can activate the epoxide, and amine functional groups play a vital role in CO2 adsorption.

head.ce:title: Functionalization of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot: A sustainable carbon-based catalyst for the production of cyclic carbonate from epoxide and CO2

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of organic compounds were successfully immobilized on an N-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD) to prepare a multifunctional organocatalyst for coupling reaction between CO2 and propylene oxide (PO). The simultaneous presence of halide ions in conjunction with acidic- and basic-functional groups on the surface of the nanoparticles makes them highly active for the production of propylene carbonate (PC). The effects of variables such as catalyst loading, reaction temperature, and structure of substituents are discussed. The proposed catalysts were characterized by different techniques, including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), field emission scanning electron microscopy/energy dispersive X-ray microanalysis (FESEM/EDX), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), elemental analysis, atomic force microscopy (AFM), and ultraviolet–visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopy. Under optimal reaction conditions, 3-bromopropionic acid (BPA) immobilized on N-GQD showed a remarkable activity, affording the highest yield of 98% at 140°C and 106 Pa without any co-catalyst or solvent. These new metal-free catalysts have the advantage of easy separation and reuse several times. Based on the experimental data, a plausible reaction mechanism is suggested, where the hydrogen bonding donors and halogen ion can activate the epoxide, and amine functional groups play a vital role in CO2 adsorption.

ce:section.ce:para: Regarding the restrictions on greenhouse gas discharge into the atmosphere, there are increasing intensives for the development of effective technologies to convert CO2 into value-added products (Anwar et al., 2020). Carbon dioxide, as an abundant, non-toxic, and non-combustible gas, is considered a green carbon source for the carbonation of epoxides (Kamphuis et al., 2019). The production of cyclic carbonates is one of the exciting areas of research, because these organic compounds find immense application in industries as a polar aprotic solvent, polymer precursor, an electrolyte for lithium-ion batteries, and essential ingredients in pharmaceutical industries (Webster, 2003).

Generally, the chemical conversion of CO2 requires energy input, because of its thermodynamic stability (Aresta et al., 2014). Because of this concern, a wide range of homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts have been developed for the reaction between CO2 and epoxides to tackle CO2 stability and make this process more sustainable. Homogeneous catalysts, which are the most reported in the literature, can be classified concerning their structures, i.e., organocatalysts (Qu et al., 2021; Fanjul-Mosteirín et al., 2019), metal complexes (Aomchad et al., 2021), and various catalytic systems containing main-group elements (Liu et al., 2016). Generally, the homogeneous catalysts exhibit high catalytic activity under mild conditions. However, separation and recovery of the catalysts and the products impose extra costs on industry (Cole-Hamilton, 2003). In this regard, various heterogeneous catalysts, including immobilized ionic liquids (Yao et al., 2020), immobilized metal complexes (Balas et al., 2021), metal-organic frameworks (Helal et al. 2020), metal oxides (Heydari et al., 2020a), and different porous materials (Wang et al., 2021; Dhankhar and Nagaraja, 2020; Kumar et al., 2018) have been developed.

Earth-abundant carbon materials, especially nano-carbons as catalysts or catalyst supports, have drawn significant attention in recent years. High surface area, availability, ease of surface modification, and functionalization are characteristics of these eco-friendly materials (Campisciano et al., 2019). Various research groups have reported the great potential of graphene oxide (GO) as a catalyst owing to its unique structure and properties such as mechanical strength, thermal conductivity, low mass transfer resistance, and intrinsic functional groups (such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy) (Navalón et al., 2018). Heydari et al. (2020b) found that GO and tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB) as a co-catalyst can produce cyclic carbonates from CO2 and epoxides at 80°C and 105 Pa in high yields after 12 hr. Various hydrogen bonding donors (HBDs) on the surface and edges of GO activate the epoxide and Br− ion open the epoxy ring. In other works, the production of different types of cyclic carbonates was studied in the presence of GO and ionic liquids (ILs) (Luo et al., 2015) and GO immobilized with ILs (Xu et al., 2015b; Zhang et al., 2016). The activity of GO was further determined at atmospheric pressure and in the presence of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) as a solvent for activation of CO2 (Zhang et al., 2018). The performance of graphene-based catalysts containing incompatible acid and base sites was also investigated by Septal et al. (2016). They synthesized amine-functionalized GO and observed good results in the presence of a co-catalyst. However, few studies are focusing on single-component graphene-based catalysts. For example, a multifunctional GO catalyst comprising amine, halide ion, and silanol groups exhibited perfect catalytic activity to produce cyclic carbonates without any solvent or co-catalyst (Lan et al., 2015).

The physical and chemical properties of catalysts can be modified by adjusting the particle size at the nanometer scale. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a zero-dimensional graphene nanomaterial with size less than 100 nm and thickness less than 10 layers (Chen et al., 2018). Since GQDs shows a tunable photoluminescence (PL) emission, most of researchers have focused on application of GQDs in PL-related fields. However, additional excellent properties such as chemical stability, dispersibility in organic solvents, low cost, and low toxicity have been noticed in recent years (Bak et al., 2016). GQDs have a single atomic layer plane structure, large edge-to-volume ratio and excessive numbers of oxygen functional groups on the surface, which can provide the active sites for different reactions (Chen et al., 2018). Due to their nm size, GQDs are well dispersed in various organic solvents to allow many organic reactions and solution processes. Therefore, with further modifications and with different synthesis conditions GQDs can be properly modified (Cho et al., 2015; Fathizadeh et al., 2019).

On the other hand, N-doped carbons have also attracted significant interest in recent years. The incorporation of nitrogen generates defect sites and improves the surface energy and physical/chemical properties (Inagaki et al., 2018; Ramesh et al., 2021). There are some studies on the N-doped carbon catalysts for CO2 capture and conversion into the cyclic carbonates (Ma et al., 2014; Molla et al., 2016; Samikannu et al., 2019). Most recently, it was demonstrated that coordinating of atomic zinc on a N-doped graphene sheet with a co-catalyst results in a very efficient catalyst toward cycloaddition reaction (Wang et al., 2019). However, N-doping is size dependent. As the size of a particle increases, the doping reaction rate decreases, and the amount of doped nitrogen atoms relative to carbon content decreases (Das et al., 2015). Therefore, it seems that N-doping enables GQD to become an interesting metal-free catalyst for CO2 capture and conversion. Wu et al. (2016) have shown the ability of N-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD) to reduce CO2 to value-added chemicals like CO, C2H4, C2H5OH, and so on. But the catalytic activity of N-GQD compounds toward the carbonation of epoxides have not been determined.

Herein, new multifunctional N-GQD compounds are synthesized, characterized, and used for the production of propylene carbonate (PC) from propylene oxide (PO) and CO2, under relatively mild reaction conditions. To the best of our knowledge, for the first time, N-GQD has been chosen to be functionalized by some brominated organic compounds. Our previous study has shown that these brominated organic compounds are highly efficient homogeneous catalysts for the cycloaddition reaction (Gorji et al., 2021). Therefore, immobilization of these compounds on a suitable support such as N-GQD may lead to an active heterogeneous catalyst. The surface of proposed metal-free catalysts contains a lot of acidic- and basic-sites and bromide ions, and they catalyze the reaction without the need for any co-catalyst or solvent. Furthermore, the effects of catalyst amount, reaction temperature, and type of functional group were also investigated. An extensive comparison among different carbon-nitrogen-based catalysts, reported in the literature, revealed an excellent performance for the proposed catalysts.

All the chemicals and solvents were used as received without further treatment. Propylene oxide (Merck), Graphite (fine powder extra pure <20
μ
m, Sigma Aldrich), sulfuric acid (95-97 wt.%, Merck), phosphoric acid (85 wt.%, Fisher), potassium permanganate (Sigma Aldrich), hydrogen peroxide (33 wt.%, VWR International), ammonia solution (25 wt.%, Acros Organics), hydrochloric acid (37 wt.%, Merck), dry acetonitrile (Solvent purification system, Vacuum Atmospheres Company (VAC)), ethanol (Honeywell), 2-bromoethylamine hydrobromide (BEAH, Merck), 3-bromopropionic acid (BPA, Merck), 3-chloropropylamine hydrochloride (CPAH, 98 wt.% Acros Organics), 1-bromopropane (BP, Sigma Aldrich), 3-bromopropylamine hydrobromide (BPAH, Sigma Aldrich), dimethylformamide (DMF, Fisher), and tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB, Sigma Aldrich).

Graphene oxide was prepared based on the improved Hummer's method (Marcano et al., 2010). At first, 1 g of graphite powder was dispersed in a mixture of sulfuric acid and phosphoric acid (130:14 mL) and stirred for 2 hr. Then 6 g of potassium permanganate was slowly added into the mixture during 4 hr, while keeping the temperature lower than 10°C by an ice bath. The obtained dark green solution was sonicated using a bath sonicator for 1 hr, and then the reaction mixture was warmed to 50°C for 48 hr to ensure that the graphite oxidation was completed. In the next oxidation step (Kang et al., 2016), the mixture was cooled down to room temperature, and 130 mL of ice water was poured into the resulting solution, and hydrogen peroxide was added dropwise until the reddish-brown solution turned into yellow. The solution was further stirred for 2 hr, and then the solid was separated from the liquid by centrifugation while the supernatant was thrown away, and solid was subjected to repeated washing. The final solid was washed with 100 mL hydrochloric acid (10 vol.%), 100 mL ethanol and distilled water several times to reach an almost neutral pH. In the end, the obtained dark brown GO solution was sonicated using a bath sonicator for 3 hr to have more exfoliated sheets (Mellado et al., 2019). The resultant GO solution (8.3 mg/mL) was quite stable in 250 mL of water over two months.

A mixture of 6 mL of GO (8.3 mg/mL), 2.34 mL of 25 vol.% ammonia solution, and 7 mL of H2O2 were mixed in a 25 mL Teflon line autoclave and heated at 180°C for 3 hr (Zhu et al., 2014). The autoclave was allowed to cool at room temperature and the solution was transferred to a falcon tube to centrifuge. A bright yellow supernatant was separated, filtered by 0.1
μ
m syringe filter, and stored as N-GQD, and the dark brown graphite-like precipitate was discarded. The as-prepared N-GQD was dispersed very well in water. The residual ammonia in N-GQD solution was removed using a rotary evaporator. The final solution was dried by freeze-drying method for roughly 48 hr, obtaining 30 mg of N-GQD. It should be mentioned that the light yellow powder particles of N-GQD adsorb the ambient moisture quickly and stick together. For this reason, all the collecting and weighing processes of N-GQD were performed in the glovebox.

In this step, the functionalized N-GQDs were obtained by quaternization reaction of 100 mg of N-GQD and excess amounts of 2-bromoethylamine hydrobromide (BEAH) in 100 mL of dry acetonitrile. The reaction mixture was stirred at 115°C under reflux conditions for three days. Finally, the solid was separated and rinsed three times with acetonitrile to remove the unadsorbed or weakly adsorbed BEAH, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60°C for 6 hr. The same procedure was used for other functionalizing agents (BP, BPA, BPAH, CPAH). The reaction sequence and possible structure of functionalized N-GQD are illustrated in Fig. 1
.

The catalysts were characterized by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, STA 449 F3 Jupiter, NETZSCH, Germany), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Alpha FT-IR Spectrometer, Bruker, USA), atomic force microscopy (AFM, NTEGRA spectra, NT-MDT, Russia), field emission scanning electron microscopy/energy dispersive X-ray microanalysis (FESEM/EDX, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV-Vis, T90 UV/Vis Spectrometer, PG Instruments Limited, UK), elemental analysis (Vario MICRO cube, HANAU Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH, Germany), dynamic light scattering (DLS, NanoPlus Series, Particulate Systems, USA), and photoluminescence spectra (PL, CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer, Agilent, USA).

All the reactions were performed in a 25 mL stainless steel autoclave in a batch mode (Anhui Kemi Machinery Technology Co. Ltd., China) equipped with a mechanical stirrer, heating jacket, temperature controller, pressure gauge, safety valve, and internal cooling coil. The thermocouple was inserted inside the autoclave to measure the reaction temperature directly. In a typical run, 40 mg of catalyst and 28.5 mmol of propylene oxide were charged into the autoclave. Before starting the reaction, the reactor was purged three times with CO2 gas to eliminate contaminants and other unwanted gases. The CO2 pressure and temperature of the reaction system were then adjusted to desired values. The agitation rate was set at 600 r/min. Upon completion, the autoclave was cooled in an ice bath, and CO2 gas was slowly exhausted. The reaction was monitored by gas chromatography/mass spectroscopy (6890N/5973 equipped with an HP-5MS UI column, Agilent, USA). A calibration curve was generated by different concentrations of the product and trichlorobenzene as internal standard. The product yield was obtained by Eq. (1).
(1)
Yield
(
mol . %
)
=
n PC
/
n 0
×
100 %
where
n PC
(mmol) and
n 0
(mmol) represent the amount of PC and the initial amount of PO, respectively.

The FTIR spectra of GO, N-GQD, BEAH, and N-GQD-BEAH were collected by Alpha FT-IR Spectrometer Bruker, and shown in Fig. 2
. In the higher wavenumber region of the spectra, the broad peaks at 3392 and 3416 cm−1 in GO and N-GQD, respectively, are related to the OH vibration in C-OH bond or physisorbed water molecules (Rui et al., 2012). The new peak at 3178 cm−1 in N-GQD spectrum can be attributed to the stretch vibration of N-H bond. The prominent peaks at 2979 and 2848 cm−1 correspond to the sp3 C-H (C-C-H) stretching vibrations in N-GQD. In the middle of the spectrum, the absorptions at 1733 and 1621 cm−1 in GO originate from C=O stretching vibration and aromatic C=C stretch, respectively, which are shifted to 1641 and 1571 cm−1 in N-GQD (Li, 2020). The FTIR spectrum of GO in the fingerprint region, including 1384, 1242, and 1087 cm−1, are assigned to the C-OH bending, C-O phenolic group, and C-O-C (epoxy) stretching vibration, respectively (Dimiev and Eigler, 2016). A new peak at 1415 cm−1 in N-GQD, related to the symmetric stretching of COO−, showed a surface with oxygen-rich functional groups. Two other sharp peaks at 1306 and 1076 cm−1 possibly belong to C-N stretching in aromatic amine and C-O vibration, respectively. The presence of nitrogen groups discloses the successful doping of nitrogen in N-GQD. The effective functionalization of N-GQD with BEAH compound was also verified by FTIR results. As shown in Fig. 2 , the bands at 2998, 1554, and 1435 cm−1 can be assigned to the C-H stretching of -CH2, N-H bending, and C-N stretching, respectively. The BEAH spectrum is entirely consistent with the literature (Appendix A Fig. S1).

The C, H, N, and S contents of different samples were measured by elemental analyzer. The results are presented in Table 1
. As is observed, the amount of carbon in GO was 41.26 wt.%, and there were some sulfur impurities. As reported by some research groups, removing sulfur impurities from GO is very hard even by repeated water washing (Dimiev et al., 2012). Increasing the nitrogen content of N-GQD from zero to 20.10 wt.% showed that nitrogen was successfully introduced into the graphene oxide structure. Table 1 indicates that, as expected, the carbon content of N-GQD was lower than GO. After functionalization of N-GQD with bromine-containing compounds, the amount of all elements in Table 1 was decreased, except for the bromine element. CHNS analyzer cannot detect the halogen elements, therefore the bromine contents for N-GQD-BEAH and N-GQD-BPA were measured by an EDX analyzer.

The microstructure of N-GQD was characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM, NT-MDT NTEGRA spectra., Russia). AFM images in Fig. 3
a and b show the N-GQD spherical nanoparticles, with an average size of about 12 nm without severe aggregation. The topographic heights in the range of 1-2.5 nm, indicating that most N-GQD nanoparticles have few layers of graphene planes (Hu et al., 2013).

The N-GQD sample was further characterized by UV-Vis spectroscopy (T90 UV /Vis Spectrometer PG Instruments Limited, UK). As illustrated in Appendix A Fig. S2a, N-GQD aqueous solution has two absorption bands at 239 and 346 nm, similar to the previous studies (Qu et al., 2015; Gökalp et al., 2020). The band at 239 nm is related to
π
−
π
*
transition of aromatic C=C bonds in sp2 domains, and the other band at 346 nm is attributed to
n
−
π
*
transition of C=O or conjugated C-N/C=N. In addition, photoluminescence (PL) spectra of N-GQD in deionized water were recorded by Agilent Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (USA), at 300, 350 and 400 nm excitation wavelengths. As can be seen from Appendix A Fig. S2b, the sample shows a green light emission under UV light and a PL peak around 505 nm at different excitations. It should be noted that the emission color of the graphene quantum dot depends on its particle size (Qu et al., 2015; Kim et al., 2012).

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted under nitrogen atmosphere by Netzsch STA 449 F3 Jupiter Thermal Analyzer, Germany. The mass change of substances was monitored at the temperature range of 25-800°C and a heating rate of 10°C
/ min
. TGA curves in Fig. 4
, clearly show the thermal behavior of N-GQD before and after the functionalization procedure. Initial 2% weight loss below 100°C for N-GQD was likely related to the release of adsorbed water. The highest thermal degradation (about 71.8%) was in the region of 100-500°C, which is assigned to the oxygen-containing functional groups and other volatile gaseous products present at the edges and basal planes of N-GQD (Li et al., 2014). Considering an additional 8.2% weight loss at above 500°C, the total weight loss was 82%. The low thermal stability of N-GQD indicates that this compound cannot be an appropriate catalyst or support for reactions at temperatures higher than 100°C. Upon functionalization of N-GQD, there is a noticeable change in thermal decomposition behavior. It can be seen that the initial decomposition temperature increased from 100 to 220°C, indicating the grafting agents were successfully attached to the N-GQD surface and their chains play a protective role and, therefore suppress the decomposition of N-GQD. The rapid weight loss around 500-600°C, which is very similar for different functionalized catalysts, is likely attributed to the decomposition of the grafting agents.

The as-prepared GO, N-GQD, and N-GQD-BEAH samples were imaged with a Hitachi S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM), and the results are illustrated in Fig. 5
. Images were acquired both with secondary electrons (SE) and with transmitted electrons (TE). For SE imaging, some samples were coated with 3 nm of Au-Pd alloy to make the surface more conductive. It is apparent from Fig. 5
a and b that GO has a two-dimensional layered structure with crumpled and wrinkled sheets. The high transparency of the image ( Fig. 5
b) indicates that we have synthesized a few-layer GO. This type of structure is quite flexible, and its active sites are more accessible for reactions (Alam et al., 2017). The microstructure of N-GQD in Fig. 5
c shows tiny particles ranging from 5 to 15 nm in diameter. It is pretty likely that the small-sized N-GQDs stack together and form larger particles (Safardoust-Hojaghan et al., 2017). The size distribution of N-GQD nano-sheets was also measured using a DLS instrument (NanoPlus Series, Particulate Systems, USA). As indicated in Appendix A Fig. S3, the average size diameter of N-GQD in water is 12.2 nm, which confirms the above results.

It seems that the N-GQD-BEAH particle size is smaller than N-GQD (see Fig. 5
d and e). The BEAH molecules may decrease the van der Waals forces between N-GQD particles by increasing the interlayer distances (Mahalingam et al., 2021). The presence of Br was verified with energy dispersive X-ray microanalysis (EDX) measurements performed with an Oxford INCA 350 EDX spectrometer connected to the Hitachi S-4800 SEM ( Fig. 5
f).

Multiple active sites on the as-prepared compounds, including HBD, halogen ion, and nitrogen-containing functional groups, are thought to be essential requirements for cycloaddition reactions (Lan et al., 2015; Ravi et al., 2018; Velázquez et al., 2017). The coupling of CO2 and PO to provide PC was considered a model reaction, and some preliminary experiments were performed to evaluate the catalytic ability of compounds in the presence of DMF as a solvent. The results of these experiments are reported in Table 2
. As is evident from Table 2 , DMF alone without any solid catalyst results in a yield of 7.4% (Aresta et al., 2003). Upon adding 10 mg of GO, the product yield increased up to 20.3%, owing to the presence of surface oxygen-containing groups of GO (Zhang et al., 2018). In Run 3, by using N-GQD, the yield increased to 31.4%. However, according to Fig. 4 , N-GQD is not relatively stable at temperatures over 80°C. It is generally accepted that the most efficient catalysts for cycloaddition reaction include a Lewis acid to activate the epoxide and a Lewis base to open the ring of the epoxide. A higher density of active sites on N-GQD-BEAH surface in Run 4 resulted in a significant yield improvement (46.75%) as compared to that of Run 3. Therefore, it seems that the functionalized N-GQD, i.e. N-GQD-BEAH, is a promising heterogeneous catalyst for the production of cyclic carbonates.

The reaction temperature and amount of catalyst are two influential factors on the reaction yield. For this reason, screening experiments were conducted to identify the most proper reaction conditions. Fig. 6
a shows the effect of catalyst loading of N-GQD-BEAH on the product yield at 140°C. Increasing the amount of catalyst leads to more exposed active sites, which in turn improves the catalytic activity. However, there was a minor enhancement in the product yield when the loading changed from 2.35 to 4.6 wt.% (81.4% to 84.24%). Hence, it was decided to use 2.35 wt.% of the catalyst for further studies to keep the catalyst consumption near the optimum value. Fig. 6
b illustrates how rapidly the product yield changes with temperature. A drastic increase in product yield was achieved at 140°C (81.4%) compared to 105°C (10.54%). Further temperature increase to 150°C causes only a slight improvement in the catalytic activity, implying that the temperature of 140°C is sufficient to obtain a high yield of PC.

In order to better understand the effect of different functional groups on catalytic performance, several types of functionalized N-GQDs were utilized with and without DMF. The results are summarized in Table 3
. It should be noted that DMF was initially used to promote the dispersion of catalysts in PO and probably activate CO2 (Wang et al., 2014). However, no evident changes were observed between the two yields in the presence or absence of DMF ( Table 3 ). All the synthesized catalysts can activate PO and CO2 simultaneously. Therefore, our proposed catalysts have successfully catalyzed the cycloaddition reaction under solvent-free and co-catalyst-free conditions. Functionalization of N-GQD not only permits the incorporation of halogen ions into the structure of the catalysts, but also increases the functional group density. The order of catalytic activity found for the production of PC was N-GQD-BPA > N-GQD-BPAH > N-GQD-BEAH > N-GQD-CPAH > N-GQD-BP. The higher performance of N-GQD-BPA may be ascribed to the higher concentration of HBDs on the catalyst surface. The carboxyl groups at the free end of the chain are more flexible than other HBDs, so they can readily activate PO molecules and facilitate their reactions with the adsorbed CO2. In other words, the pendant alkyl chain of BPA on the surface may presumably decrease the distance between active sites (Baj et al., 2014). Furthermore, we investigated the effect of amount of N-GQD-BPA, with and without DMF solvent, on the product yield (Appendix A Fig. S4). It seems that DMF only has a positive impact on the yield at catalyst loadings lower than 2.35 wt.%. However, this positive effect becomes marginal at higher catalyst loading.

Immobilization of BEAH and BPAH onto N-GQD increases the concentration of amine functional groups on the surface, which in turn improves the CO2 adsorption capacity. According to Table 3 , a longer alkyl chain of BPAH in Run 4 led to lower product yield as compared to BEAH in Run 1; because BPAH may create steric hindrance around the active sites and suppresses the access of epoxide and CO2 molecules to the surface (Baj et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2015a). The worst catalytic performance was found for N-GQD-BP in Run 3. It seems that, the BP alkyl chain without any functional group, can move freely around the surface and cause steric hindrance and, therefore decrease the catalytic activity of the active sites present on the surface of N-GQD.

The type of halide anion is another crucial factor in cycloaddition reactions. As reported by some research groups (Seo and Chung, 2014; Della Monica et al., 2016), Br− is more active than Cl−, because of the higher nucleophilicity and better leaving group ability of bromide as compared to those of chlorine (Wang et al., 2006). Thus, it may be concluded why the N-GQD-CPAH catalyst in Run 5 is inferior in the catalytic activity as compared to the one utilized in Run 1.

The catalytic performance of various carbon-nitrogen heterogeneous catalysts is summarized in Table 4
. We have collected and presented a comprehensive data available regarding the reaction conditions and activity of the carbon-nitrogen catalytic systems.

As can be seen in Table 4 , the best catalytic systems that resulted in large product yield above 98%, need either or both of co-catalyst and solvent. A high yield was obtained for the catalyst in entry 7 under relatively harsher reaction conditions. However, the presence of transition metal ions like Zn2+ and their influence on catalytic performance should not be ignored. The activity of our organic catalysts in entries 20-22 with no solvent, co-catalyst, or transition metals are comparable to other studies. The PC yields for N-GQD-BPA and N-GQD-BEAH reached 98% and 93%, respectively, at a longer reaction time.

In order to examine the stability of the proposed catalysts, several experiments were carried out with N-GQD-BEAH and N-GQD-BPA. After the reaction, the catalyst particles were collected by high-speed centrifugation and washed with acetonitrile followed by vacuum drying at 60°C. In general, catalyst deactivation is unavoidable for most catalytic processes. Catalyst deactivation originates from various sources such as thermal degradation, poisoning, leaching of the active phase, etc. However, in most cases, a single mechanism cannot explain the decrease in catalytic activity, and catalysts usually experience several deactivation processes in the course of chemical reactions (Hammond, 2017). The results of the stability tests are shown in Fig. 7
. Based on four catalytic cycles, the proposed catalysts are relatively stable with no measurable loss of activity.

We think that the high density of functional groups and bromide ions on the surface may provide required active sites for the PC production from PO and CO2. According to the present and previous studies (Lan et al., 2015; Samikannu et al., 2019; Baj et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2021a, 2021b), a possible reaction mechanism is proposed for the reaction catalyzed by N-GQD-BPA in Scheme 1. In the first step, a hydrogen bonding occurs between PO and carboxylic acid group on the edge plane, which can produce complex II. Subsequently, the CO2 molecule is adsorbed and activated by nitrogen functional groups to form carbamate species. This is followed by the attack of Br− as a nucleophile to the less substituted carbon of epoxide (III). The interaction between these two species results in complex IV stabilized by a hydrogen bond. Subsequently, the cyclic carbonate is formed upon ring-closing.

A group of multi-functional graphene-based catalysts was explored to catalyze the reaction between PO and CO2 at relatively mild conditions. A variety of functional groups were designed on the surface of N-GQD to prepare a so-called one-component catalyst. Synergistic effects of hydrogen bonding donors, amine functional groups, and halogen ions, present on the surface of the proposed organocatalysts, vastly improve the catalytic activity. The factors influencing the cycloaddition reaction were studied, and a possible reaction mechanism was proposed. The N-GQD-BPA catalyst exhibited superior performance for the production of PC, which may be related to the high density of carboxyl groups. Various environmentally-benign features of the current catalytic systems, such as the absence of metal, solvent, and co-catalyst, reusability, and sustainability, open intriguing possibilities for the future development of organocatalysts.
